content.claim: Students from higher socioeconomic backgrounds are significantly more likely to access the selective early-academic tracks in Hungary even after controlling for prior ability; these tracks show higher value-added in literacy and tertiary progression, thereby increasing inequality of opportunity.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Selection into tracks may be influenced by unobserved family factors not fully captured by ability controls
Cross-sectional stratification does not prove causality of the track structure itself versus broader institutional factors
Value-added estimates depend on model specification and baseline assumptions
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
